content.plain_english: This week your child is measuring how long and how tall things are. They start by comparing two objects, then move on to using a ruler with centimetres.
content.in_school: Children begin by holding two pencils side by side to see which is longer. They measure with hand-spans and cubes, then with a ruler — saying things like 'this pencil is 12 cm long'. They put three or four objects in order of length.
content.home_activity: After bath time, hand your child a ruler or tape measure. Measure their teddy bear in centimetres. Then measure your hand. Whose is longer? By how much? Two minutes — the conversation matters more than precision.
dinner_table_questions: Is the spoon longer or shorter than the fork?
Roughly how many hand-spans wide is the table?
Can you guess how tall a milk carton is in centimetres?

key_vocabulary.definition: How long something is from end to end.
How tall something is from bottom to top.
A small unit for measuring shorter things — about the width of a fingernail.
A straight tool with cm marks for measuring.
Look at two things to see which is longer or shorter.

example_questions: Which is taller — a teaspoon or a teapot?
Use a ruler to measure your pencil in cm.
Order these from shortest to longest: 8 cm, 14 cm, 3 cm.
content.times_table_tip: Year 1 children practise counting in 2s, 5s and 10s. Many longer rulers also show 5s — count up in 5s along the ruler: 5, 10, 15, 20.
